> What about adding a func for furl to realize remove('one').add({'one': 'one'})
> When query amount is multi, this will be very useful.

There's another, straightforward way to accomplish the same task without
enumerating the keys twice: remove the entire query with
`furl.remove(query=True)`.

For example:

```python
>>> from furl import furl
>>> f = furl('http://www.google.com/?one=1&two=2')
>>> f.remove(query=True).set({'new': 'arg'}).url
'http://www.google.com/?new=arg'
```

Does `furl.remove(query=True).set({...})` suffice for your needs?

This is one major problem of PyPower. The Algorithm does not detect branches without anything connected.
What I'm trying to say is that when the last Bus of a strand has a total value of (0+j0)MVA the Jacobian cannot be inverted.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Simple Fix is to change all 0 values for P in ppc bus array to 1e-10
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
What should be done though is to automatically detect these branches/buses/generators and deactivate them. Before building the Jacobian.

It looks like aiodns/c-ares simply return unqualified domain names. This
is, they return a domain that it's not fully qualified or complete up to
the top of the DNS namespace (the root, ".").

ns1.google.com and ns1.google.com. don't have to be the same domain
necessarily, but they will indeed be the same pretty much most of the time.
For most user oriented DNS tools it is safe to strip the last zone
separator (".").

If the fact that labels returned by aiodns are unqualified is a problem and
you need to programmatically fully qualify them in the standard DNS root,
use your favorite DNS library to canonicalise them before querying them*.

If you simply need to print them (you're not parsing the string and turning
it into a domain object) perhaps appending a trailing dot would suffice?

* I don't know what's your use case so I can't offer much advice

auto_threshold_image was never very great.  It's depreciated and you should use parition_pixels, which is now the default method used by any routine in dlib that requires a threshold.  So for instance, if you just called threshold_image and didn't supply a threshold it would invoke partition_pixels to find one.  

So to summarize, no one should use auto_threshold_image anymore.  partition_pixels is way better and gives a much better thresholding value while being more general and robust.

Let's unwrap the call. This creates the object with a UDP socket prepared for network communication:

    udp.UdpSocketTransport()

Then this binds the socket to specific interface/port:

    udp.UdpSocketTransport().openServerMode(('localhost',1))

By this point I/O object is ready for communication. But we need to tie it up with SNMP core so that it could pass SNMP packets to the I/O object and vice-versa:

    registerTransport(udp.domainName,  udp.UdpSocketTransport().openServerMode(('localhost',1)

The `domainName` constant is an ID for this I/O object (UDP over IPv4) at the SNMP core. When you ask SNMP core to send SNMP message to another SNMP system across the network, you will use that `domainName` ID to direct SNMP core towards specific network transport to use. It is not directly relevant to the network address which you will also provide.

I'm not convinced that having two separate arrays makes it really that much easier. @reyale, you say that it would allow this interface:

```python
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_csv('data.csv')
result = df[(df['lat'] > 40) & (df['lon'] > 40)]
Layer(data=[result['lat'],result['lon']])
```

However, the current interface allows very similar behaviour:

```python
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_csv('data.csv')
result = df[(df['lat'] > 40) & (df['lon'] > 40)]
Layer(data=[result[['lat', 'lon']])
```
... which seems just as easy.

Similarly, if the data is in two lists:

```python
Heatmap(data=zip(latitudes, longitudes))
```
... is very easy to use.

Ultimately, what makes the array of *latitude*, *longitude* pairs most appealing to me is that a pair is really a single object. A latitude is meaningless without a longitude (in terms of expressing the location of a point on the globe). I think of the `data` array as a single array of points, rather than an array of pairs of floats. 

I think that we are really optimising for two different things. The proposed new interface may be superior for someone dealing entirely with tabular data. The current interface optimises for conceptual simplicity.

Hi all -- NoBrokersAvailable is raised in this scenario if bootstrapping metadata failed. Bootstrapping relies exclusively on `bootstrap_servers` . If you are seeing this error it is because kafka-python was unable to connect to any of the brokers you configured as bootstrap_servers.

If you want to find out more about what is happening under the covers, enable debug logging:
```
import logging
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG)
```

In addition there will be some additional connection logging in the next release and that should make it even easier to diagnose networking problems like this.

pyodbc uses SQLGetDiagRec (ANSI) API to retrieve the error message. The SQL Server driver is a Unicode driver, with SQLGetDiagRecW only, so the DM will attempt to convert the returned message into the current ANSI encoding. If the server message is in Japanese and the latter is e.g. 1252, there will be characters which can't be converted, hence the ???? in the message.

The fix is to use SQLGetDiagRecW instead and retrieve the message (UTF-16) into a Unicode Python string. This is not difficult; I will work on making a fix for it.

My problems with your suggestion that `IDF.copy` should be the name for `IDF.deepcopy` and that we remove `IDF.(shallow)copy` are that a) it runs counter to what an experienced Python developer would expect and b) it leaves us in a situation where `idf.copy()` and `copy(idf)` do different things. This is an area, like with `IDF.printidf()` where there is a native Python way to do things (`print(IDF)`) and an Eppy way to do things which runs slightly counter to the Zen of Python, but worse here if they don't do the same thing. I think the only reason we're justified having it at all in this case is that `deepcopy(idf)` fails on Python. Perhaps a better course of action is to figure out why `deepcopy` is trying to operate on a closed file in Python 3 and fix that instead.

Ok, so the process I followed is listed below. The most relevant steps are 5 and 9 where the bulk of the changes for cross-version compatibility were made. Most of the rest was to make the CI work. In going through the changes just now I've spotted that `pip install eppy` won't work cross platform with environment markers, so there's a new commit where that's fixed that too.
 
1. Use environment markers to install version-specific packages when installing, e.g:

>pydot>1.0; python_version <= '2.7'
>pydot3k; python_version >= '3.0'

2. Removed most of `p3` directory and adjusted CI specifications to stop changing to the `p3` directory when running under python 3.

3. Added `six` to `requirements.txt`

4. Removed the `install_requires` section from `setup.py` since everything is in `requirements.txt`

5. Run tests under Python 3, fixing failures as they happen. Diff between the following commits to see details of what changed. There's a good list of the features/idioms available for cross-version compatibility including using `six` [here](http://python-future.org/compatible_idioms.html):
#9b69d0
#b971bc
#afa6c0e
#931522

6. Once all tests were passing, removed the rest of the `p3` directory and `2to3.sh` files

7. Fixed one more failing test in commit #d15205

8. A bunch of changes related to CI and coverage testing

9. Ran `2to3` on the whole codebase to catch any issues in areas that aren't yet exercised by unit tests (I could probably have done this first and saved a lot of time!)

10. A few more CI-related changes

11. Fixed `setup.py` to clean up the things from item no. 4 in this list and allow `pip install eppy` to work properly for Python 2 and 3

The name `nextopen` and `self.dataclose[1]` show that this idea is not compatible with *backtrader* approach.

`next` is called to evaluate the delivered bar and this is at index `0`. The previous bar is at index `-1` and the one before that one is at index `-2` and so until the initial bar.

Even if for performance reasons the prices under backtesting have been preloaded, looking at index `1` is out of the question because it defeats the purpose: *making an evaluation based on something which is yet to happen*, in this case the next *opening* price.

When `next` is called is called to perform the *next* evaluation (in contrast to the last which was already done). The current and last available price is, again, at index `0`.

Without knowing what exactly `order_target_percent` does behind the scenes, it cannot for sure make a calculation based on a not yet available price (in this case `nextopen`).

The next `open` price can only be known when it happens.

If you use daily bars:

  - `next` evaluates a closed bar and an order set during the evaluation will be executed with the next bar. You can make an educated guess as to how much the *opening* price will gap if you set a market order, but the order can be rejected if too many shares are requested or fall short of coming close to your 100% target.

If you use sub-day bars:

  - `next` will see the open price and you can decide if you enter in the next sub-day bar with a `Market` order (which again may not meet your expectations) or you can set a `Limit` order for an exact 100% investment but fail to be matched at the requested price.

But magic doesn't exist and when the market is closed the `nextopen` price cannot be known.

Sorry Yoanis, but not yet. If you just want to use the current version that is on PyPI, you simply need to ignore the FixturesMixin completely. Just decorate your class with the `fixtures` decorator function and don't worry about inheriting from the mixin class. Now, if you're curious as to why the change...

So, the current version that is on PyPI is perfectly usable, but it has some limitations as to what I can do with it, hence the need to change it. Specifically, I want to be able to use the the decorator to mark individual methods with fixtures data to be setup and torn down before and after the test method runs. With the current version of the library, I can't do that, and so I'm changing it to use a metaclass instead of a decorator for all of the internal class manipulation that it's doing. I originally set out on this path to begin with, but I had this brilliant idea that I could do what I wanted without metaclasses, but unfortunately, I was wrong. In the new version, you'll inherit from the mixin class and identify the fixtures files you want to load through a pair of class variable's: `fixtures` and `class_fixtures`. For method specific fixtures, you'll then be able to use the decorator to mark the method as using a specific set of fixtures files. So, it will be slightly backwards incompatible, but it will be much more flexible and powerful going forward, and the changes that you'll need to make to your code when switching between versions is fairly trivial.

If you'd like to use the new version, you can do so, though it's not as thoroughly tested yet as the one in PyPI, but it should be fairly reliable. To use it, you'll have to pull it directly from github. You can do so with the following command:

```
pip install git+https://github.com/croach/Flask-Fixtures.git
```

I hope that helps. I should have the new version published to PyPI within the next few weeks, I just have a few more TODO's to check off before it goes live.

One advantage is that magic-wormhole can provide a secure channel after exchanging _only_ the wormhole code (a short string like "3-aardvark-component") between the two parties.

If the two parties have already exchanged an SSH key (or have a convenient, secure way to do so) then scp or rsync (over SSH) provide very similar security properties.

magic-wormhole may eventually include some NAT traversal functionality which would provide some additional convenience beyond those SSH-based solutions by automatically jumping through the extra networking hoops needed to get traffic to someone behind NAT (but it's certainly also possible to apply such techniques to an SSH connection and perhaps there are other existing tools which do so).

Reading this I am not convinced the docs are the first thing that needs consideration.

In my view, languages should have concepts that are orthogonal to each other. For every concept, it should work everywhere, and it should always work in the same way, no matter what other concepts are used around it.
Unfortunately, in real life, concepts are often not entirely orthogonal, for various reasons. In that case, I prefer explicitness. I like the tool to complain about my impossible wish, rather than changing the semantics. I would be forced to explicitly write the two rules that you proposed as solution.

The disadvantage of this approach is that a user hits the limits of the tool a little earlier, but it's a clean limit. Either the input is accepted and each concept works as advertised or it fails.
You can push the boundaries a little further like you do in the combined named-assignment-priorities-terminal-rule case, but I got confused, and reported it as a bug. Right now, you're considering how to explain this all in the documentation. In my view, it's all muddy water of edge cases with weird highly complicated semantics depending on the exact combination of concepts that only you can truly grasp.

Things I would consider:
- Implement named assignments for terminal rules. They may not make much sense, but if it's easy to support, and a user sees a use for it apparently, why not?
- Forbid the combined named assignments and priority for terminal rules. They make no sense, don't try that! Simplest is forbid feature A if the feature B is also present, so in the docs, you can explain how to do the combination near the forbidden feature A.

I think the problem is that while that file exists, there will be a [compiled `.pyc` file](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/2998215/if-python-is-interpreted-what-are-pyc-files) which is used instead, so no matter what you edit the `.py` file to, your changes wont be loaded.

I would recommend that you can just create a file called **myfont.py** (or something, in the same directory as your program) with your custom font definition called `MY_FONT`, and then in the program:

```python
from myfont import MY_FONT

...
show_message(device, msg, fill="white", font=MY_FONT)
```

Hence, shouldn't need to alter the existing font.py in luma.core.

There is none.
Direct hash are just here to give some authentication class examples.
You should write you own authentication class implementing https://github.com/nitmir/django-cas-server/blob/master/cas_server/auth.py#L37

You can always subclass `SqlAuthUser` or `LdapAuthUser` (I do not known which one you are using) and just change the `test_password` method to suit your case.

I do not intend to add salt options for plain hash algorithms as some people may hash differently (with the salt before or after for exemple) with the salt stored differently (in another field, on the first bytes of the password hash, with a separator in the password hash, ...). Contrary to the the [crypt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crypt_(C)) format which is well documented.
This is fixed in `0.7.0`

Well after much fiddling around, the explanation turns out to be quite simple. When opening the file with PIL `Image.open(f)` the file pointer is advanced but not reset before writing the file. I would think that in this case the file pointer would reach the end of the file and no bytes would be written, but it doesn't seem so based on my previous partial writes. In any case, the solution is to rewind the file pointer to the start of the file before saving:
```
f.seek(0)
f.save(os.path.join(imgdir, filename))
```

```

engine = create_engine('sqlite:///:memory:', echo=True)
Session = sessionmaker(bind=engine)
Base = declarative_base()


class Parent(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'parent_objs'
    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    obj_type = Column(String, nullable=False)
    name = Column(String)
    __mapper_args__ = {
        'polymorphic_on': obj_type,
        'with_polymorphic': '*'
    }


class Child(Parent):
    __tablename__ = 'child_objs'
    id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey('parent_objs.id'), primary_key=True)
    columnA = Column(String)
    _sum = Column(Integer)

    }


class Related(Base):

    num = Column(Integer)

Base.metadata.drop_all(engine)
Base.metadata.create_all(engine)

session = Session()

c2 = Child(columnA='some2')

rc2 = Related(num=2)

c2.related.append(rc2)

session.add(c1)
session.add(c2)
session.add(rc1)
session.add(rc2)
session.commit()

expr = func.sum(Related.num)

query = query.select_from(inspect(Related).selectable)
query.correlate(Child)

condition = join(

).onclause
query.where(condition)
print("\n\n=================\n\n"+str(query)+"\n")

update_query = Child.__table__.update().values({
    '_sum': query
}).where(Child.id.in_([1, 2]))

print("\n\n=================\n\n"+str(update_query)+"\n")

On line 38 I was trying to substitute a no-op lambda function for the `lru_cache` decorator, which isn't present in earlier versions of Python, but I overlooked that  `lru_cache` is a function that *returns* a decorator (see [the docs here](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functools.html#functools.lru_cache)).

Here are two quick workarounds (choose one):

a) Install [backports.functools_lru_cache](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/backports.functools_lru_cache/1.3) and change line 38 to:

    from backports.functools_lru_cache import lru_cache

b) Change line 38 to:

    lru_cache = lambda: lambda func: func

Not using `lru_cache` will have a slight perform impactm but only if you have a *very large* config file.

If that's what you mean: The way pydocmd works at the moment, this is not possible. It simply imports your module and then looks at its contents. There's no order in maintained when you import a module. It would probably be possible if we'd parse the module into an AST and use that to figure out the order of functions that are being declared. If that should be made possible at some point, I think it should be configurable and not be the default.

Afaik other documentation generating tools also don't mind the order of definition in the source file. I would definitely agree that the order should be consistent, though (eg. alphabetical).

Hi, @Marigold!

Sorry for late response.

Short answer: use native interface `uri = 'clickhouse+native://default:@localhost/test'` and it'll be fine.

Long answer. Pandas `read_sql` method actually emits two queries:
* `EXISTS TABLE  select day, value from rate limit 2;`
* `select day, value from rate limit 2;`

There are two interfaces supported by `clickhouse-sqlalchemy`:
* HTTP. It is the first one, but it has some limitations. For example we need to add `FORMAT TabSeparatedWithNamesAndTypes` for parsing on the python side. This interface is default now. This behavior will be changed in future releases.
* TCP. This is the same interface used by `clickhouse-client`. This interface is more flexible. It appeared few moths after HTTP interface was implemented in this library.

In case of HTTP interface following SQL is rendered: `EXISTS TABLE  select day, value from rate limit 2; FORMAT TabSeparatedWithNamesAndTypes`. Trailing semicolon doesn't make any sense, query will fail even without it. See ClickHouse logs `/var/log/clickhouse-server/clickhouse-server.log`. In `read_sql` terms this table doesn't exist if `EXISTS` query is failed.

You should use TCP interface. It hasn't problems with FORMAT clause.

I've managed to remove the `root`/`sudo` requirement from most of the builder. However, one piece is still remaining, and that's these lines of code: https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/18cf1a99d333bde630f1bd3bad8029752d1799a9/builder/image/templates/builder#L101-L105

We cannot run `chown -R 2000:2000` unless we're root, since we would be giving away the file to another user, which is forbidden - https://superuser.com/questions/697608/chown-operation-not-permitted

I solved this other places by adding the `git` user to the `slug` group, and then do something like this:
```
    # give slug group ownership of TMP_DIR and CACHE_DIR. This is used by the
    # slug builder. Contents in these folders will be owned by slug:slug.
    chown -R :2000 $TMP_DIR $CACHE_DIR
    chmod g+rwx $TMP_DIR
    chmod g+rwx $CACHE_DIR
```

(which works). However, that only works since I know that TMP_DIR and CACHE_DIR will be created as `git:git` and I can then move it to `git:slug`. `/buildpacks` could be owned by root or some random uid/gid, and then I'm out of luck.

I can add `chown -R 2000:2000 /buildpacks` as the single root command allowed to execute for `git` as `sudo`, but I'm wondering if anyone has any better alternatives?

Hey, sorry for the delay here! It's been a crazy few weeks.  Firstly thanks for starting this very necessary discussion and tackling this documentation problem!

@kwill : 

> --system just says "System pip management." This is less clear to me. What does it mean?

It just means we use `which pip` to find pip and invoke it directly, basically, instead of making a virtualenv (so often this is derived from `python -m pip` or else it is found on your path).  It means that installation is done without any resolution and without any virtualenv creation.

>     pip(env[sic]) install with a combination of --system and/or --deploy
>     AND, I have a requirements.txt in my root folder
> 
> ...will I reimport the requirements.txt? I still have it for legacy reasons, but I don't want it to interfere with my pipenv-driven deployment.

If you have a pipfile or a lockfile, pipenv should skip your requirements file if it finds one.  If you use `--deploy` pipenv should never use anything but your lockfile and will fail spectacularly if the lockfile and pipfile do not agree with one another (the lockfile stores a hash of the pipfile used to generate it)

----

@sirosen 

> There appear to be at least three ways of doing a reproducible install from the lockfile. Which of these is recommended, and why?
> 
>     pipenv install --ignore-pipfile

This installs dependencies from your `Pipfile.lock` only, whether you have a pipfile or not, whether it is out of sync with your lockfile or not, it doesn't even check.

>     pipenv install --deploy

This command is an 'enforcement' command, it basically says 'install the dependencies from my lockfile, if and only if my lockfile is in sync with my pipfile, otherwise give me an error message'.

>     pipenv sync

This command says 'take the dependencies specified in my lockfile and put them in my virtualenv'

It's weird to have site-packages in PYTHONPATH. Normally Python can find its own site-packages automatically.

Many Python developers do not use PYTHONPATH at all. Instead, put a .pth file in the site-packages directory of the Python installation that you intend to use. Here is a blog post: http://bob.ippoli.to/archives/2005/02/06/using-pth-files-for-python-development/

That doesn't seem to fix it, because that commit just adds handling of bytestrings.  Bytestrings already work in Python 2.  The problem is that in Python 2, `str` is a bytestring, and `unicode` is a separate type.  So the checks in various places that do things like `if isinstance(blah, str)` fail if `blah` is actually a unicode object.

In the case of that hypothetical general purpose solver, 'x' and 'y' are the correct dimension names, since they could represent anything. They have to be overridden by the model script to have a meaningful name depending on the context.

Also, when you download the class_labels.txt file linked in the page https://apple.github.io/coremltools/, it gets downloaded as a file named "synset_words.txt" so you should use that name instead, i.e.:

```
coreml_model = coremltools.converters.caffe.convert(
('bvlc_alexnet.caffemodel', 'deploy.prototxt'),
class_labels='synset_words.txt'
)
```

If the user running conda doesn't have write permissions to paths it needs to modify in the environment, then conda can't do anything.  (We can do better with error messages though.)  When conda encounters a permissions error like this during an install/uninstall transaction, the best case scenario is for conda to roll back all disk-mutating actions its made up to that point--and that's exactly what happened.  Conda behaved correctly here.

Without your `conda info` output, I can't give you specifics, but whatever environment is active during your `conda update anaconda` command has some messed up file ownership.  This most commonly happens when you use `sudo` for commands that operate on that environment.  To work your way out, you'll probably have to use `sudo chown -R` on the environment.

@mcg1969 I think that's great. I've been wanting to do this for a long time (https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/634 https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/454), and have mostly been blocked on a slow solver. **But**, as I noted, you can't impose this restriction unless the solver is able to solve for removed packages. As a trivial example, as soon as someone `conda remove`s a package that is a dependency their environment will become inconsistent (albeit typically in a trivial way). 

As a more complex, but also quite common example, someone who installs anaconda and wants to update, say, Python to the next minor version. Right now anaconda 2.5.0 depends on Python 3.5.1. When Python 3.5.2 comes out if someone who installed anaconda runs `conda update python`, under this scheme, it won't update it. If they run `conda install python=3.5.2` it will give unsatisfiable package specifications of `python` and `anaconda`. The only way for this to be completely smooth, from a user point of view, is if the solver can realize that `anaconda` is not important and can be removed. `anaconda` is a simple, and common example, but there are others as well. Typical examples are now outdated packages that were installed automatically at some point, but are not needed any more, but would restrict what can be installed due to their dependencies (say, a package that is no longer supported and was only built against an older version of NumPy).

Finally, some people "work around" inconsistent dependencies by doing the install in two stages (e.g., a package isn't built against the version of numpy you need, but the build that exists turns out to work just fine, so you install and then manually change the numpy version from under it).

If this is already done, let me know (I haven't been following the new solver developments very closely). 

My suggestions if it isn't:

- Guess what packages are important to a user from the history file.
- Allow a "version" of a package to be installed in the solver to be "removed" (or "not installed"). It's an exercise for the reader as to what priority that should have over the other versions. 
- Only enable this feature optionally, at least at first (perhaps on a per-environment basis). But don't enable it by default, until you have a grounding on what the implications are.

Apparently, this StackOverflowError happens when unpickling 32-bit Python pickle files on a 64-bit Java: https://github.com/jpmml/jpmml-converter/issues/4

The solution is to upgrade to 64-bit Python. Technically, it should be possible to add support for 32-bit Python pickle files, but it's not a priority.

> Do you have any example PMML file which uses this?

Integration tests for the `KMeans` estimator type use cluster affinities for "external" validation:
https://github.com/jpmml/jpmml-sklearn/blob/master/src/test/resources/main.py#L112-L118

So, instead of saving cluster affinities to a CSV file, they should be bundled into the PKL file so that the JPMML-SkLearn library could see them.

> Are you planning to implement this feature in sklearn2pmml?

Not a priority for me.

But lets keep this issue open, as a means to track ideas/progress on this topic (and related topics).

On further review, it looks like it will not be possible to implement a FIDO-based SSH agent with the current SSH agent protocol and expectations. There's neither a way in FIDO to sign raw data (without `authenticatorData` concatenated to the beginning) nor a means in the SSH agent protocol of providing any context around the data that was signed (e.g. that it was prefixed with a counter).

If either of these could budge over time, it would be a nice step toward inexpensive, easy-to-use, hardware-protected authentication for SSH. In any case, I'll close this for now.

That is, **if** you have an editor config plugin for your editor, it will observe the rules in the `.editorconfig` file.  However, you could still violate the whitespace rules if you don't have an editor that observes the suggestions in the `.editorconfig` file.

The `.gitattributes` approach **enforces** these whitespace rules through git itself, so as you observed, you get modified files on checkout solely because git enforced `.gitattributes`.

I haven't reviewed the diff, but if it's just whitespace changes and it makes git happy, then go for it :smile_cat:

Especially in light of #354, I've been looking at this again.
I'd like to get it merged at some point, but we need to add a little bit of compatibility glue to keep `globus whoami` sane in cases where the user has already done a `login` with the older scopes.

A crude spec of what we need to add:
- `globus login` should explicitly delete obsoleted config data on success
- if the `whoami` info is present in config, `globus login` should behave as though it got `--force`
- `globus whoami` checks config data and works the old way...
  - unless you gave `--linked-identities`, in which case it fails and demands a fresh login
  - or if there is no config data, pursues the new codepaths

I'd also like input from @jaswilli on what other cases we might need to handle. This was all I could think of.

The logic in `class_::dealloc` is fine: values with `return_value_policy::reference` are not owned, so calling either the destructor or `delete` will result in double-frees.

The issue here is specific to `std::enable_shared_from_this` since `init_holder_helper` will increment it's reference count even for `return_value_policy::reference`. The solution should then be either:

1. Set `owned = true` to correctly decrement the refcount. However, this does essentially violate `return_value_policy::reference` since we are taking ownership.

2. Keep `owned = false` but avoid incrementing the refcount in the first place. This could be done by skipping `init_holder` for certain return value policies. However, I'm not sure how this would play out with the different combinations of return value policies, holder types, `type_caster_holder::load()` and `type_caster::load()`.

This strikes me as fairly heavy weight (code added to every function dispatcher). Can't we just point out in the docs that you're not supposed to mix holder types? It strikes me as a fairly rare design pattern to use ``unique_ptr`` and ``shared_ptr`` interchangeably for a type.
This also fixes a non-rvalue forwarding (though I'm not sure it caused any practical problems).

from zmq.utils import jsonapi

Currently, there is no syntax to specify attributes within a native Python data structure. Instead, you need to use the `set_xml_attr()` method on an existing `XMLDictNode` instance.

```
user@h0$ cat set_xml_attr_example.py 
#/usr/bin/env python
import jxmlease

data = { 'Monkey': {
             'Name': 'Lando Griffin'
         }
       }

result = jxmlease.XMLDictNode(data)
result['Monkey'].set_xml_attr('key','value')
print(result.emit_xml())

user@h0$ python set_xml_attr_example.py 
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<Monkey key="value">
    <Name>Lando Griffin</Name>
</Monkey>
```

Alternatively, you can specify the `tag` and the `xml_attrs` arguments to the `XMLDictNode()` constructor.

```
user@h0$ cat xml_attrs_example.py 
#/usr/bin/env python
import jxmlease

data = { 'Name': 'Lando Griffin' }

result = jxmlease.XMLDictNode(data, tag='Monkey', xml_attrs={'key': 'value'})
print(result.emit_xml())

user@h0$ python xml_attrs_example.py 
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<Monkey key="value">
    <Name>Lando Griffin</Name>
</Monkey>
```

> I agree that scale is a better name.

Note that this would also mean different semantics. At least for me `scale=2` would imply upscaling the image, not downscaling.

> The output shape is calculated from the input shape, but height and width has to be fixed otherwise the location network will not work i think?

The localization network doesn't have to use the same input as the transformer network, e.g., it could use a version scaled to a fixed size, or different features altogether.

> Even if you end in a dense layer, you still need a shape that remains constant across examples. [...] But I have a feeling there's a misunderstanding here.

Yes, we're mixing two things here :)
a) Do we need to support a scaling factor at all, or is an output size enough?
b) Is there any benefit from computing the output size symbolically, from a factor given at construction time?

I was referring to your following comment, which I interpreted to address the first point:

> So I don't really understand how being able to specify a scale factor exempts you from knowing the input shape -- you still need to know what shape the output of the TransformerLayer is going to be anyway in most cases.

If the TransformerLayer is not the first thing in your pipeline, then you don't necessarily know the input shape, and you don't necessarily have a particular output shape in mind, but maybe you have an idea about what would be a good factor (or you can try a few). So I'd like to keep support for a scale factor.

About the second point, you wrote:

> But a fixed shape is what you want, no? Except in the special case where the network you stack on top of it is fully convolutional and you have some kind of global pooling layer at the end.

Yes... I agree that's a very unlikely use case. The TransformerLayer might actually be more useful to bring images from a variable input size to a fixed output size (although that would limit the ability to do batch processing). But apart from a lack of use cases, is there a good reason for not supporting the combination of `input_shape == (batch_size, channels, None, None)` and a factor? It doesn't really complicate the code if we agree we want to support both factor and size specifications.

We use marathon and the upgrade strategy deal with this problem.
https://mesosphere.github.io/marathon/docs/deployments.html#rolling-restarts.
This will kill half your existing workers, deploy new code and wait for
them to come up and own a partition and report healthly, and then finally
marathon will replace the remaining workers with the new version.

If you have more then one partition in a topic this will allow the balanced
worker to keep processing during your deployment and protect you from
deploying completely broken code.

```python
import pykafka
import time


client = pykafka.KafkaClient("localhost:9092")
topic = client.topics["test-big"]
producer = topic.get_producer(delivery_reports=True)

payload = 800 * 1024 * "_"
for _ in range(10):
    producer.produce(payload)

for _ in range(10):
    msg, exc = producer.get_delivery_report()
    assert exc is None
    print time.time()
```

There is a way around this, and that is to compile Curator.

I just built this and verified it against a clean VM with nothing pre-installed.  You don't even necessarily need to have python installed at all, since it's bundled.

# Compile Curator into a binary with included libraries

## Errata

**NOTE:** This how-to is only verified for CentOS 6.  **There are extra steps/workarounds involved to enable cx-Freeze on other platforms.** Also, this build is x86-64 only.  You should be able to modify the steps/VMs to work for other platforms, but that is not covered here.

This requires either `vagrant`, or a CentOS 6 installation with internet access.  These instructions use Vagrant, so you can build it anywhere

### Make a curator build directory (replace `/path/to` with whatever you would like, that you have access to):

```
mkdir /path/to/curator_build
```

### Build a Vagrant VM:

```
mkdir centosVM
cd centosVM
```

### Put the following in a file called Vagrantfile:

```
Vagrant.configure(2) do |config|
  config.vm.box = "elastic/centos-6-x86_64"
  config.vm.synced_folder "/path/to/curator_build", "/curator_build", create: true, owner: "vagrant", group: "vagrant"
  config.vm.provider "virtualbox" do |v|
    v.customize ["modifyvm", :id, "--nictype1", "virtio"]
  end
end
```

Note that `/path/to/curator_build` is in this configuration.  Be sure to replace it with whatever you used in that previous step.

### Run:

```
vagrant up; vagrant ssh
```

### Inside the VM, run: 

```
sudo -i
yum groupinstall "Development Tools" "Development Libraries" python-devel
pip install -U setuptools

wget https://github.com/elastic/curator/archive/v4.1.1.tar.gz 
tar zxf v4.1.1.tar.gz
cd curator-v4.1.1
pip install --user cx-Freeze
pip install --user -r requirements.txt

python setup.py build_exe
mv build/exe.linux-x86_64-2.6 /curator_build/curator-4.1.1
```

### Test the compiled version from this directory:

```
cd /curator_build/curator-4.1.1
./curator --version
```

You should see:
```
curator, version 4.1.1
```

### Exit the vm now:
```
shutdown -h now
```

### Conclusion
When you exit the VM, the fully compiled-for-CentOS 6 version of Curator will be available in `/path/to/curator_build/curator-4.1.1`

You can now tar/zip this directory and copy it to the target machines.  You can also delete the entire VM directory afterward.

If you want to build `primer3` w/ a non-gcc compiler you'll probably have to modify the Makefile (`primer3-py/primer3/src/libprimer3/Makefile`) to add the appropriate compiler flags to avoid these compilation errors. Looking closer at the trace this may be a simple out-of-memory problem. 

If I'm reading this correctly, the stackoverflow post that you cite has nothing whatsoever to do with the problem you are seeing right now. It just happens to mention Java and segfaults. Is that correct?

mpz_manager::big_set is a very old and well tested function and I wouldn't expect a segfault to originate there. However, it (de-) allocates pieces of memory when numbers get bigger (smaller), so I would expect that either allocation fails or that some part of the memory has been corrupted before (e.g. a double free).

Since OSX does not come with Java: Which version of Java are you using? From the version strings in the crash output, I assume it's Oracle Java 1.8.0_60, but it may well be that some other implementation tries to mimic Oracle Java. 

The Oracle Server VM (which is indicated by the crash output) uses the concurrent garbage collector by default, so there could be a problem that is concurrency related and that you wouldn't see on another OS that perhaps uses the non-server VM by default. Are you using any kind of concurrency/parallelism on your side?

By default, Java VMs are not allowed to use all of the system memory, that requires options and the amount and options may have to be set up differently for different systems or Java implementations. So without further information, I'd guess that the JVM is out of memory but the native code doesn't throw a nice exception (which may or may not be fixable from our side).

Thanks for the ticket, this is a good idea. I am still unsure how to implement that, I have two ideas:

### Global setting in the radio
The number of retry is set globally un the radio driver:
``` python
import cflib.crtp
from cflib.crazyflie import Crazyflie

cflib.crtp.radiodriver.set_retries_before_disconnect(1500)
cflib.crtp.init_drivers()

cf = Crazyflie()
cf.open_link("radio://0/42/2M")
```
Advantage:
 - The setting is for the radio link only, it does not apply to other link
 - One Global setting

Drawback:
 - Cannot be set per Crazyflie connection

### Setting in the Crazyflie
The setting is set in the crazyflie before connection:
``` python
import cflib.crtp
from cflib.crazyflie import Crazyflie

cflib.crtp.init_drivers()

cf = Crazyflie()
cf.set_retries_before_disconnect(1500)
cf.open_link("radio://0/42/2M")
```
Advantage:
 - Expose less 'implementation details' since the setting is generic and does not target the radio
 - Setting can be set for each crazyflie independently

Drawback:
 - Does not make sense for any other link than the radio so having it generic is missleading (ie. this makes not sense for USB link).

Looks like there is a bug in s3 provider; however, I think the setup that you are describing (where you have an API server working on localhost) won't work even if there was not one - because GitHub authentication is locked to a particular url (in our case, zoo.studio.ml); and if you want to use GitHub auth with your own api server, you'll need to create a new GitHub app and put your own secret environment variables when deploying the API server. I realize docs are lacking on that subject, so I'll add that soon. 
Back on the topic of the problem though - if having your own api server is not a requirement, you can use it in a serverless fashion (i.e. both studio runner and UI can talk directly to s3 to get and put experiment data). This can be achieved by using the config that I provided above both for `studio run` and `studio ui` commands (this config: 
```
database:
    type: s3
    bucket: elejke-studio-meta
    authentication: github

storage:
    type: s3
    bucket: elejke-studio-artifacts

server:
    authentication: None

verbose: error

resources_needed:
    cpus: 2
    ram:  3g  
    hdd:  60g 
    gpus: 0
```
This way, you won't have an API server, but you should be able to both run and see experiments in the UI. 

If a private API server is a requirement, then for the moment please consider using Google storage as a provider (then the database section of the server config should be something like
```
database:
   type: gs
   bucket : my-metadata-bucket
   authentication: none

server: 
    authentication: none
```

What it does, is it listens to what happens in the network, filters the traffic so that only what goes to/from backblaze is considered and it saves that to `.pcap` files for analysis later. Additionally, it cuts each packet just after 68 bytes, so no actual data is collected, only the packet headers.

If this is running when you have an error, then the packet traces can be opened using a special program (usually Wireshark) and a human then can see what was going on.

Network administrators are known to actually react sometimes if you show them a packet dump and a screenshot made with wireshark, showing that something is not working correctly.

The error you are seeing is coming from Cassandra itself: you are trying to create a keyspace with a replication factor of 2 in a cluster with only 1 node. While that is possible (if you are going to add more nodes afterwards), it is rarely the correct thing to do, and Cassandra-migrate tries to execute migrations with consistency level `ALL`.

You either need to add more nodes to your local cluster (ideally 3 to be able to form a quorum) or reduce the replication factor to 1. The load-balancing policy warning is inconsequential and unrelated to the error you are seeing.

I created #38 to track adding support for setting a custom consistency level. If you really did mean to create a keyspace in a partially configured cluster, please add a comment there to let me know.

hi, 
thanks for the answer (and sorry for taking so long to respond)
the examples i provided have just been the most minimal examples i could
think of that would reproduce the problem. (which resulted in using only
a single array 'a' for all inputs out of lazyness)

my actual task can be described as follows:

> i need to evaluate a list of ~10-20 rather lengthy functions (>10 000 characters as string)
> that are all structured in such a way that CSE would definitely make a lot of sense...
> the functions have at least 4 input-arguments that will be either scalars, 1d- or 2d arrays
> (depending on user-input) and thus i need reliable array-broadcasting to actually evaluate them.

for the time being i got back to using symengine just to perform the symbolic evaluation 
of the functions, and then sympify the results to use sympy's cse and lambdify to generate 
my own version of cse-based functions which ended up being a more robust and fast 
approach than using symengines Lambdify-possibilities to take over the evaluation.

the major drawback here is that sympifying takes up to some minutes to convert the 
derived symengine-based formulas back to sympy-based formulas, but symengines power in 
performing fast symbolic equation-expansions still pays off...

i will keep an eye on the development of symengines Lambdify-possibilities, and as soon
as the broadcasting-rules can be considered "save" i'll give it another try...

We have a spinel property `THREAD_RLOC16_DEBUG_PASSTHRU` to enable RLOC16 traffic (which basically disables the filter).  And the documentation in [spinel-doc](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/doc/draft-spinel-protocol.txt#L2413) mentions default should be `false` (meaning rloc 16 pass-thru is disabled or filter is up)...

My suggestion is to follow this and enable the filter by default and use the property  to change the behavior when it's needed.

To actually fix this import order dependence (if a file from a pantsbuild.pants package is imported from pantsbuild.pants.controb.scrooge and sibling files in the package only exist in pantsbuild.pants because scrooge does not use them - boom) this issue will need to be fixed.

If this issue will take more time to resolve than is desirable for you (I expect this will be the case), then an option I support would be to make an auxiliary target - say in contrib/scrooge/BUILD - that depends on both src/python/pants:_pants_transitional_publishable_binary_ and  contrib/scrooge/src/python/pants/contrib/scrooge/tasks and src/python/pants/goal:task_registrar and has a setup_py.  Burning and using that 1 sdist would work around.

Looking into a bit further, it seems like this is a bug with bottle. We're calling `bottle.run(app=app, host=c.host, port=c.port, server=c.server)`. There seems to be an _old_ bug in gunicorn referenced at bottlepy/bottle#756, found at benoitc/gunicorn#91. Apparently gunicorn creates an ArgumentParser and pulls in the arguments with which the calling script was called.

I'm not sure what we can do about this on our end, as it's going to require a patch to the way that bottle calls gunicorn. In the meantime, it's probably best to run pypiserver directly via the gunicorn server as described in the docs.

I understand that is not straightforward to decide the best way to plot the cumulative daily returns plot when the forward returns have periods greater than 1 day (as the forward returns of subsequent days overlaps), but I noticed the code saves the forward returns for any period length as **daily** returns (please see #74) so the returns are already in a format suitable to be passed to the  plotting function (plotting.plot_cumulative_returns_by_quantile). Plotting the daily returns should be a reasonable compromise.

The other, more complex, option would be to calculate the daily returns as the mean of all the overlapping returns that influence that day: for a period of N days each daily returns  would be calculated as the average of N forward returns (day 1 returns of '1 day ago' factor, day 2 returns of '2 day ago' factor, day 3 returns of '3 day ago' factor and so on until N day ago factor). But that wouldn't give us much more information. 

This also applies to  #65.

So, the issue is the following.

The global scheduler attempts to load balance tasks across all of the machines, and it has two sources of information for tracking the load on each machine.
1. It receives periodic heartbeats (every 100ms) from each local scheduler saying how many tasks are queued or executing on that machine.
2. For each local scheduler, it keeps track of how many tasks it assigned to that local scheduler since it received the last heartbeat from that local scheduler.

So suppose that the global scheduler assigns a task to one machine. If the local scheduler simultaneously sends a heartbeat with its load information to the global scheduler, the global scheduler will forget about the task it assigned once the heartbeat arrives.

So in practice, if heartbeats are received while the global scheduler is handing out tasks, the global schedulers load estimates can be inaccurate which can lead to poor load balancing.

There's a separate issue, which is that tasks assigned locally (without going through the global scheduler) do not count toward the global scheduler's bookkeeping until the next heartbeat, so in the meantime the global scheduler can have a slightly stale world view.

I was going to suggest solving this by doing the following:
- Don't let tasks be scheduled locally (e.g., on the node that you run the driver from, start that node with `--num-cpus=0`).
- Pause long enough during scheduling to allow the global scheduler to receive up-to-date load information from the local schedulers.

However, that doesn't quite work because the information sent by the heartbeats and used by the global scheduler is mostly about the number of tasks queued on the node and not the number of available workers.

We'll need to give this more thought, but can probably come up with a short-term fix.

Note that even with poor scheduling, `ray.wait` can be used to drop straggler tasks.

@istoica For the job API, the Spark submit is a similar one. And Ray should provide a http server to user can submit, view, cancel the jobs. Another required important feature about job is separate resource manager for different user to avoid one user occupy all the resources in a cluster. If the feature(resource isolation) is not a general one, we need the interface in the job management module to let us apply our internal policy.

For the LRU, different job's life cycle is different, many small jobs create many temp variables and exit, and few large job run a long time. So the objects created by the long run job will be created will be deleted because its' life time is longer than the objects' created by the small jobs.

We did consider to create a new GC algorithm to resolve the issue. Ray has provide the interface, it will modify the code less. But we can't find a good solution to our scenarios. LRU is a GC algorithm, the GC algorithm is not better than java GC algorithm. It can't provide the scope resource management just like RAII in c++ and using in C#.

If you have concern on add top level API for RAY, with the job level support, we can add a flag to submit to let user decide recycle the objects created in the job or not. It is more nature to user, and will not expose the details in the job. 

Spill over the objects to a persistent store is an option, but it can't resolve the issue because many temp variables are useless when a job exit in graph computation. The objects inflation can't be avoided in graph computation. In our case, 1 node will access the 300 neighbor nodes, it will be done for 3 times, which means 300^3 temp variables will be created. After a job exit, we don't want to maintain these objects. 

And in our current thinking, spill over the objects to persistent store need job level support. Retired objects in a job will be put to the persistent store as a group, and we will not keep entries for these retired objects in the plasma store. We also are trying to apply the ray graph to online calculation scenarios now, the perf of it is very sensitive, we only have less than 300ms budget, large page out and page in to persistent store is not acceptable.

@raulchen yes, this is rare. Your proposed solution sounds right. We would also want to periodically clear the (object_id, client_id) wait list.

But this may make sense coupled with a change to the code at task forwarding time (in the node manager): If we push task dependencies (the objects) to the node to which a task is forwarded without checking whether the object is local, some of those objects may never be local on the node that is forwarding the task. Additionally, with such changes to task forwarding, some objects may appear locally after the call to push: If we invoke an actor method, the task may be forwarded before the object store notification is received by the node manager. We currently check that an object is local before pushing, which sometimes fails to push objects for which an add notification hasn't yet been received. See https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/src/ray/raylet/node_manager.cc#L821.

I think Jonathan is right and there is data leakage. I actually ran into this problem myself when implementing something similar. I decided to ignore this for now for several reasons:

1. Others found it had little impact on overfitting (e.g.  from a top kaggler's [blogpost](http://blog.kaggle.com/2016/12/27/a-kagglers-guide-to-model-stacking-in-practice/)):
> This is leakage and in theory S could deduce information about the target values from the meta features in a way that would cause it to overfit the training data and not generalize well to out-of-bag samples. However, you have to work hard to conjure up an example where this leakage is significant enough to cause the stacked model to overfit. In practice, everyone ignores this theoretical hole (and frankly I think most people are unaware it even exists!).

2. If you want to avoid it the evaluation process requires (potentially a lot) more computation time. Consider training a StackingEstimator with K-Fold you propose, in this case the estimator will generate out-of-sample predictions for each row in your data (so without data leakage), and the rest of the pipeline can then use this to make further predictions. This means instead of training one model, you trained K models. You need to do this for each fold in the cross validation of your pipeline (by default, 5). 
Then, consider adding another StackingEstimator, SE A, before your first StackingEstimator, SE B (i.e. the pipeline is now (SE A)->(SE B)->(final estimator)). SE A needs to provide predictions for SE B, for each fold SE B uses to create predictions. SE A can't simply be trained in K folds on all of the data, because then it would leak some data for SE B (i.e. if SE B makes predictions on a certain fold, SE A can not have been trained on any data of that fold). This means that to provide SE B with leak-free predictions, it needs to be trained K times for each time SE A is trained. So we see an exponential growth in the number of models to be trained to create a leak-free estimate (i.e. K^S where K is the number of folds you use per stacking estimator, and S is the number of sequential estimators). Especially in an evolutionary algorithm where we want to evaluate hundreds to tens of thousands (or more) pipelines,

3. While the stacking estimator is trained with this leak, the evaluation of the entire pipeline will still be on out-of-sample rows. This means the StackingEstimator's final model will not actually be trained on data of that fold. The leakage might introduce a bias to use more of the StackingEstimator's predictions, but if they turn out not to generalize well, they will soon enough be dropped.

All in all, in theory, I do think you should avoid this issue. In practice, I am not sure the benefits will outweigh the costs.

I'm also facing this issue.

Considering a 10% `PercentageDiscountBenefit` offer on a product with a 20% `FixedRateTax`.

1. Discount amount is computed [here](https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/1.1.1/src/oscar/apps/offer/benefits.py#L71-L72), we get `price` from `get_applicable_lines`
2. `get_applicable_lines` retrieves price on `utils.unit_price` ([here](https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/1.1.1/src/oscar/apps/offer/abstract_models.py#L604)), itself using `line.unit_effective_price` ([here](https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/1.1.1/src/oscar/apps/offer/utils.py#L20))
3. `unit_effective_price` get the `effective_price` on `purchase_info.price` and `purchase_info` has the strategy applied ([here](https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/1.1.1/src/oscar/apps/basket/abstract_models.py#L733-L734))
4. Having a strategy based on a `FixedRateTax`, the `TaxInclusiveFixedPrice` is used ([here](https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/1.1.1/src/oscar/apps/partner/strategy.py#L266-L269)) so `effective_price` is **tax included** ([here](https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/1.1.1/src/oscar/apps/partner/prices.py#L96))

That far, everything seems fine, our discounts are based upon tax inclusive prices, that seems in accordance with `TaxInclusiveFixedPrice` pydoc ([here](https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/1.1.1/src/oscar/apps/partner/prices.py#L79-L81)) added in @codeinthehole's commit a737f74 which is precisely related to this "tax in/ex-clusive offer situation".

The fact is I don't get why discount is computed like this:

```python
def apply_discount(line, discount, quantity):
    """
    Apply a given discount to the passed basket
    """
    line.discount(discount, quantity, incl_tax=False)
```
With a forced `incl_tax=False` in `offer.benefits` ([here](https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/1.1.1/src/oscar/apps/offer/benefits.py#L19-L23))

Because of this, the discount amount, computed on the **inclusive price**, is set to the `_discount_excl_tax` attribute of the basket line ([here](https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/1.1.1/src/oscar/apps/basket/abstract_models.py#L646)).
Then, when we check for `line_price_excl_tax_incl_discounts `, the [errored discount amount is returned](https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/1.1.1/src/oscar/apps/basket/abstract_models.py#L765-L767).

Like @mbertheau, I'm not well aware of other countries tax application. Setting `incl_tax=True` in `apply_discount` fix it for my situation but I don't truly realize the implication with other benefit types.

Whatever, there's definitely an issue there: discount amount is computed from an inclusive price and then retained on an exlusive price. This is not related to any country law and sounds like an insidious silent bug which can only work if the tax is deferred and then not taken in account in `unit_effective_price`.

Hi @kooba 

You're solution is indeed ok and it is documented like this in the djangorestframework documentation.

However, to solve this properly you need to fork the basket app from oscar just to override the `LineAttributeModel` (https://django-oscar.readthedocs.io/en/releases-1.5/topics/fork_app.html), and you needed to override oscarapi's serializers.

You could solve it as well by just overriding oscarapi's serializers (see https://django-oscar-api.readthedocs.io/en/latest/usage/customizing_oscarapi.html), and do something like this:

```python
from oscarapi.serializers import LineAttributeSerializer, BasketLineSerializer


class MyLineAttributeSerializer(LineAttributeSerializer):
    code = serializers.SerializerMethodField()

    def get_code(self, obj):
        return obj.option.code


class MyBasketLineSerializer(BasketLineSerializer):
    attributes = LineAttributeSerializer(
        many=True, fields=('url', 'option', 'value', 'code')
        required=False, read_only=True)
```
Good luck!

Hi. I was looking for a way of _creating_ a DOTX file using `python-docx`. This Issue was the closest reference I found to my problem.

I would like to leave my solution here, in case somebody else has the same problem:

```python
import docx
document = docx.Document()
document.add_paragraph('Helloworld.')
document_part = document.part
document_part._content_type = 'application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.wordprocessingml.template.main+xml'
document.save('foo.dotx')
```

Conversation about Unlock / UnlockTransfer vs. Secret:

Jabob:```
> How do you guys feel about renaming the “Secret” message to “Unlock”?
I was thinking about it, and it makes sense to rename. My reasoning is:

The secret message is used to perform an action of unlocking the asset locally and withdrawing in the counter party end, in that respect the Secret is just the data used to perform unlock/withdraw.

But I'm not sure if Unlock is the best name.
```

Ameen:```
My reasoning for calling it “Unlock” or “UnlockTransfer” is that in addition to just the secret, for the “Secret” message to be a valid BalanceProof, it needs to update the nonce, locksroot, and transferred_amount

And my implementation plan right now involves creating an “UnlockTransfer” message and registering it on both the sending and receiving sides, much in the same way that a DirectTransfer would be created and mutually registered.

I’m open to keeping it at Secret, but then we have to call the registration method something else so it doesn’t get confused with “register_secret"

My plan so far was to go with “create_unlocktransfer” and “register_unlocktransfer”
```

I agree it sounds rocky, but in the context of the app it makes sense. We have an app with a django user model that is backed by LDAP, so the username field is the 'true' primary key and the integer id is an implementation detail for that application. The Emberjs app pulls in data from this system and two others, so exposing the primary key seems risky as its just the ID for this particular row, the username is much safer to share between the systems.

Also we view exposing the user id as a security risk, it opens the system up to easy enumeration attacks whereas the username is not susceptible without a large brute forcing effort. Upgrading the central app would be a large undertaking, and we've been using this method for a while without any issues with the DRF ember package.

Surely it's not too much to just use the `id` field specified in the serializer, it seems a weird limitation to have.

I have a general question (after reviewing the pull request) about this approach of Kerberized mechanism for the following 2 scenarios-

Scenario one:
===========
When SparkMagic is being used inside JupyterHub then the kerberos authentication happens at JupyterHub (userid/passwd are authenticated at JupyterHub before opening any notebook) and the token (not any user-id/passwd) is normally passed to the SparkMagic for further authentication. 

Scenario two:
==========
Kerberos authentication can also being done using a keytab file which is very common in corporate environment. When you get authenticated using keytab file (using "KINIT") then you just need to pass the kerberos principal name to the HTTPKerberosAuth object inside "reliablehttpclient.py" file at "_send_request_helper" method. The above 2 scenarios doesnt need any mechanism within SparkMagic (inside notebook) context to accept userId/passwd.

This a bit late, but in the spirit of @kalouantonis comment, I wanted to share how I worked around this limitation in dumpdata.

Initial Steps (after getting this error):

1. Delete fixture record pointing to the polymorphic_ctype
2. Delete all fixture records pointing to the previous record
3. Reload fixtures
4. Re-enter the content I removed in the previous step
5. Re-dump fixtures (see below)

```
# The key option here is --natural-foreign
python manage.py dumpdata --natural-foreign --indent=4 -o ./fixtures/fixtures.json
```

[Here's the Django Docs on --natural-foreign](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/ref/django-admin/#cmdoption-dumpdata-natural-foreign)

They even mention to use this option when exporting `contenttypes`; but it's easy to miss.

Anyway, hope this helps someone!

gcloud should work asis in GAE right now, but its performance would be significantly worse than ndb's. Implementing on top of something like `datastore_query` and `datastore_rpc` would allow it to use either datastore_v1 or datastore_v3.

JJ was able to convince me that NDB should not be implemented on top of `gcloud.datastore.simple`. However, I do think that the `simple` implementation should be pretty minimal with most of the heft in `datastore_query` and `datastore_rpc`.

Importantly though, I think that `datastore_rpc` and `datastore_query` will need some work before they become part of gcloud. I'm *almost* done getting them to support both Cloud Datastore and datastore_v3. However I think they'll need some serious API cleanup before being part of the gcloud library.

Personally (I don't think we really talked about this), I would like to see them result in a nice API for library developers or for customers who want more control than ndb or simple provide. I think right now there is some overlap between the existing implementation in gcloud and `datastore_rpc` and `datastore_query`. It may be worthwhile to see how we can best merge the API of the first with more of the functionality of the second.

from import_export.widgets import Widget


class ChoicesWidget(Widget):
    """
    Widget that uses choice display values in place of database values
    """
    def __init__(self, choices, *args, **kwargs):
        """
        Creates a self.choices dict with a key, display value, and value,
        db value, e.g. {'Chocolate': 'CHOC'}
        """
        self.choices = dict(choices)
        self.revert_choices = dict((v, k) for k, v in self.choices.items())

    def clean(self, value, row=None, *args, **kwargs):
        """Returns the db value given the display value"""
        return self.revert_choices.get(value, value) if value else None

    def render(self, value, obj=None):
        """Returns the display value given the db value"""
        return self.choices.get(value, '')
```

Hello all,

@jmchilton and I had a phone chat, here is a summary:

1. This is a great proposal and everyone is welcome to assist @jmchilton in implementing it, but until it becomes part of the standard it needs to live behind a flag as a hint that this behavior isn't portable yet.  So no need to fork `cwltool`, development can happen here.

2. Speaking of becoming part of the CWL standard, the deadline for 1.0 is tomorrow, which means this is the perfect time to mature this feature for the 1.1 release!

3. CWL absolutely supports "thick" tools; I had completely forgotten about the [ShellCommandRequirement](http://www.commonwl.org/draft-3/CommandLineTool.html#ShellCommandRequirement)! Mea culpa. I edited my reply above to reflect that.

4. Now that we have more breathing room, the community can decide: do we assign one ID to a tool and map that to the different packaging systems or do we have a long, long list of IDs in every tool definition?

@sjackman If the packaging system has its own mappings for identifiers to package names then those can be used. For example, the Debian Med team is integrating the EDAM & bio.tools identifiers into their infrastructure. Alternatively the CWL implementation can maintain their own mappings, or users could provide these mappings in their job input objects. This is likely useful for systems that have no standardization (environment modules). Alternatively the packaging system can cross reference its own index of "binary" application names against the `baseCommand`
